title: Spotify rolls out its own AI-transparency feature in beta - Music Ally
text:
Last September, Spotify announced a series of measures
relating to AI music, spam and deepfakes
. They included supporting – and, indeed, revealing for the first time – a new industry standard for music credits to make it clear how and when AI is used in music tracks.
Now Spotify has provided an update on the latter plan. “Starting today, we’re launching a beta feature that allows artists to share how they’ve used AI in their music,”
the company announced yesterday
.
“Where artists have chosen to disclose through their label or distributor, you’ll see credits for specific contributions like vocals, lyrics, or production in Song Credits on mobile.”
The key part of that sentence is “chosen to disclose” though.
“Because we depend on artist disclosure, the absence of a credit doesn’t mean AI wasn’t used,” admitted Spotify. “Not all distributors enable artists to disclose yet, but we intend to expand this more broadly over time.”
When Spotify originally announced its plans for AI-transparency credits, its named partners included DistroKid, CD Baby, Amuse and Believe among other distributors. Spotify’s next step will be to lean on those partners to require disclosure.
Where does the new industry standard fit into this? Well…
“We know this isn’t a complete solution on its own. Building a truly comprehensive system is a challenge that requires industry-wide alignment, but we didn’t want to wait to get started,” revealed Spotify in its latest update.
“That’s why we’re tackling it on parallel paths: adding this information to our Song Credits, while continuing to help lead the development of a broader industry standard for AI labelling that would give consumers consistent information across all services.”
Last September, Spotify said that it was working with standards body DDEX on the new standard, although there’s no news from the latter on that – the last announcement on
its website’s press releases page
was in December 2022.
Since Spotify’s original announcement,
Apple Music has gone its own way with its ‘transparency tags’ system
– which also relies on disclosure from artists and distributors – while Deezer continues to use
its own ‘AI-generated content’ tag
based on its in-house detection technology.
It’s hard not to suspect that the “industry-wide alignment” that Spotify is hoping for may be slow in coming – or may not ever arrive.
But a more important question about the efforts of all these DSPs is this: if and when AI-disclosure becomes mandatory, how will they enforce it – and what will the punishment be for artists and distributors who remain opaque about their use of this technology?
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
